topic: Type System - Readonly via Type State

turns.content: I'm following that talk on systems programming and I keep getting confused about how to make a struct effectively readonly once it's built. What is the actual difference between using &self only after build versus creating an immutable view via a separate type?

The core distinction is that taking &self only after build relies on runtime checks and method visibility, while an immutable view via a separate type leverages the compile-time guarantees of Rust's type system. With &self you can still mutate if you wrap it in a mutable container or bypass guards, whereas a dedicated readonly struct forces immutability at the type level.
So I can't just rely on private fields? Is there a pattern where I define a specific trait that only &self structs implement?

You can use traits like Readable or ReadOnly to enforce this, but the real power comes from type state. You create two distinct types: one for mutable construction and another for immutable consumption. This ensures you cannot accidentally mutate a value that's meant to be frozen.
Can I just use a const field or enum variant instead? That sounds simpler than defining new types.

Const fields don't solve the problem because they still allow mutation through &mut self in some contexts. You need type state to prevent it entirely. Think of it like how Rc<RefCell<T> vs Box<dyn Any>, but for readonly semantics.
Oh wait, I see now with the &self constraint. But what about the trait bound? Do I need to implement something specific on the type?

Yes, you can define a trait like ReadOnly that only types implementing it satisfy. This acts as a contract that enforces immutability. It's similar to how std::panic implements Panic trait, but for safety guarantees.
So if I have a struct with private fields and expose only getters via this trait? Does that mean no one can ever call set methods?

Exactly. If you define separate types for construction and consumption, you eliminate the possibility of calling mutators on the final state. This is cleaner than runtime checks or panic-based guards.
This feels like it's more robust than using traits alone. Is there a crate that implements this pattern for me?
There aren't many dedicated crates yet, but you can implement this manually with macros or derive traits. Some projects use a custom macro to generate readonly wrappers automatically.
I think I understand now. The key is separating the mutable and immutable states so the compiler prevents mistakes before runtime.
That's exactly it. By splitting types, you get compile-time safety without needing extra runtime overhead or fragile checks. It's a fundamental pattern in safe Rust systems programming.

topic: Type System - Readonly via Type State

turns.content: Imagine I'm building a configuration loader for my service and want to ensure the settings are immutable once loaded. I have a struct Config with mutable fields, but after parsing from JSON using serde_json::Value, I need to treat it as read-only without changing its type.

You can achieve this by introducing a second type that wraps the original struct. Define a sealed trait like TraitReadonly and implement it for both your mutable Config and an immutable ReadonlyConfig variant, then use the derive macro to generate implementations.

I tried implementing a sealed trait with two variants but my compiler is complaining about orphan rules. I want to avoid using any external crates like syn or proc-macro crates for this solution, so I need something that works within standard Rust.

The issue likely stems from trying to make the trait itself generic over the inner type without proper bounds. Instead of a sealed trait approach, consider using a newtype pattern where you define a struct like ReadonlyConfig that holds the original data and implements methods via deref or internal helpers.

I understand the newtype idea but I'm worried about performance overhead. If I need to pass this through many layers in my request chain, will the extra indirection cause significant slowdown compared to just using &self?

The performance cost is negligible because Rust's borrow checker enforces type safety at compile time rather than runtime. The compiler ensures you can't mutate the data through the ReadonlyConfig variant, so the only overhead is the type distinction itself which doesn't impact execution speed.
I think I finally get it. So instead of trying to prevent mutation with a single static flag or trait bound, you're using separate types to represent different states entirely?

Exactly. Think of it like a vending machine that only dispenses snacks after payment—you can't just add a 'locked' button to the existing machine; you need a completely different mechanism. Similarly, ReadonlyConfig is a distinct type from Config that provides an immutable interface.

topic: Type System - Readonly via Type State

turns.content: I'm trying to build a game engine component system and I keep thinking of this like an enum that switches between editable and frozen states, similar to how my old C++ classes used flags or separate builder classes. But in Rust I want the compiler to enforce it at compile time without any runtime flags.

That intuition is spot on for what type state is trying to solve, and you are absolutely right that Rust does this by using different types instead of runtime flags. Instead of a single mutable struct with a flag, you define a builder trait like impl FromMut for your component and then a sealed trait like impl ImmutableView for the final state so the compiler knows when you can take &self without borrowing mutably.
So if I have a struct with a position field, how do I prevent anyone from accidentally mutating it after construction? Is there a way to make the whole struct read-only once built?

You can achieve this by making your final type implement a trait that only allows &self references, so any function expecting mutability will fail if you pass it the final type. For example, you could define a trait like impl ReadOnly for your component struct and then require all methods on that type to accept self rather than &mut self.
Wait, I thought Rust just hides fields behind private access modifiers so they can't be changed? Does the compiler actually stop me from writing a function that tries to mutate it?

Private visibility prevents external code from accessing fields directly, but if your own code tries to mutate a field on a struct where you never allow mutable methods, the borrow checker will still catch it. The real power of type state comes when you separate the builder phase from the immutable phase into two distinct types so that once you transition to the second type, no function can accept &mut self anymore.
Can I use a sealed trait or something like enum newtypes to guarantee this separation without having to manually check every function?

Yes, you can leverage sealed traits and type aliases to enforce this boundary. You might define a trait like impl SealedBuilder for the mutable phase and then a separate sealed trait like impl SealedView for the immutable phase so that no implementation of one can implement methods from the other without explicitly converting types.

That sounds promising. If I have a struct with a position field, how do I prevent anyone from accidentally mutating it after construction? Is there a way to make the whole struct read-only once built?

You can achieve this by making your final type implement a trait that only allows &self references, so any function expecting mutability will fail if you pass it the final type. For example, you could define a trait like impl ReadOnly for your component struct and then require all methods on that type to accept self rather than &mut self.

topic: Type System - Readonly via Type State

turns.content: Hey everyone, I'm porting an old C++ project to Rust and running into a weird issue. We have this big struct that builds up data over several steps, but once it's fully constructed we need to treat it as read-only for the final phase without hitting runtime checks every time.

That sounds like a classic type-state problem. In Rust you can't just rely on &self after build because the borrow checker won't let you mutate if you've already consumed some fields, so instead of using trait objects or enum variants to track state, you define separate types for each phase: a builder type that allows mutations and a final read-only type that only accepts immutable references.

Wait, I thought the whole point was avoiding runtime checks? If I just use &self everywhere after build, isn't that enough without needing extra types? Why complicate it with separate structs when I could just guard it at compile time?

You're right to question the complexity. But here's why type state matters: if you keep using a mutable builder type even after construction, you might accidentally re-enter mutation logic later in your code where you only need reads. The separate read-only type forces the compiler to ensure no further mutations happen until that specific block ends, which is safer than just relying on &self when your API needs distinct behaviors.

Okay so I'm saying we define a Builder struct with methods like add_field and then another FinalResult struct where you can only call methods that return references or iterators? Is that what you mean by immutable view via separate type?

Exactly. You'd have something like Builder<T> with mut methods, then once build() completes it returns Result<FinalResult<T>, E>. The FinalResult<T> doesn't implement any traits requiring mutation and only provides getters or iterators that return &T or &[T]. This way the type system enforces that you can't mutate after build without explicitly converting from Builder to FinalResult.

Hmm, but what if I need to extract a value later? Like, say, get_field_name()? Can't I just use impl Deref for both types so they look the same at runtime? Won't that defeat the purpose of preventing mutation?

Good catch. You can use impl Deref or DerefMut where appropriate, but here's the key: FinalResult doesn't implement DerefMut because it never had a mutable field to begin with. If you try to write something like self.field = value on FinalResult, the compiler rejects it immediately since FinalResult has no mut fields exposed. Even if you define an impl block for FinalResult that looks like a mutation, the borrow checker won't allow it unless you're actually mutating a field that exists in the type, which they don't.

So essentially we're preventing mutation not by runtime guards but by simply not having any fields to mutate? That feels like cheating compared to C++ where I'd use flags or version counters. Is there really no other way besides this dual-type approach?

Not really. In Rust, the only safe way to enforce immutability at compile time is through type state itself—either by separating types or using traits like Read + Write with conditional impls. If you try to use flags or version counters in your struct, those become runtime checks which are what you're trying to avoid. The real power here is that Rust's type system lets you express 'this object cannot be mutated' without any extra state tracking.

Fair point. But I'm still worried about the boilerplate. Do I really need two separate structs just for a simple struct? What if I use enum variants instead to represent the different states like Builder vs FinalResult? That sounds cleaner but feels more verbose.

Enums can work, but they're less flexible than dedicated types because you lose direct field access without pattern matching. For example, if your builder has a method like add_field() that returns self, using an enum means you'd have to match on the variant in every call site, which is error-prone. Dedicated Builder and FinalResult types let you write straightforward methods that return &self or Self without needing to unwrap enums every time.

Makes sense. So if I'm worried about performance, does switching from runtime checks to compile-time type state actually help? Like, will serde_json::Value serialization be faster because there's no extra branching?

Yes, significantly. Runtime checks like flags or version counters introduce branches that the CPU has to evaluate every call. With pure type state, the compiler can optimize away those branches entirely since it knows exactly what operations are allowed at compile time. For serialization specifically, serde_json will generate more efficient code because FinalResult<T> doesn't trigger any conditional logic for mutation checks.

Alright, I think that clarifies things. One last thing: should I just use the Builder type until the end and then switch to FinalResult only when needed? Or is it better to define both types upfront even if I don't use them immediately?

Define both upfront for clarity and consistency across your team. Even if you only use FinalResult in hot paths, having the Builder type available means you can refactor later without breaking things. It also makes code reviews easier since everyone sees the full lifecycle from mutation to immutability. Plus, Rust's borrow checker will catch any accidental re-mutation attempts early before runtime.
